mnemonics.title: fseek Origins
mnemonics.content: SCS - SEEK CURrent SET: SEEK_SET starts from beginning, SEEK_CUR uses current position, SEEK_END starts from end. REMEMBER: Think of 'CUR' as 'CURSOR当前位置' (current position).

acronyms.usage: fseek(fp, offset, SEEK_SET) positions offset bytes from file start
fseek(fp, offset, SEEK_CUR) moves pointer offset bytes from current position
fseek(fp, offset, SEEK_END) positions offset bytes before file end

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a arrow moving along a ruler (file bytes). fseek() jumps the arrow to any ruler position. SEEK_SET is ruler start (0), SEEK_END is ruler end, SEEK_CUR is wherever the arrow currently is.
Think of file as numbered parking slots, each exactly the same size. To find slot N, simply calculate: position = (N-1) × slot_size. No need to check slots 1 to N-1.
Binary mode: read exactly what is stored. Text mode: apply translation rules (like spell checker). For random access, binary is essential because you need exact byte positions.

analogies.analogy: Random access is like opening a book to a specific page using page numbers. Sequential access is like reading from page 1 to find page 50.
fseek() is like using the scroll bar on a document editor to jump directly to a specific line, rather than pressing page-down repeatedly.
ftell() is like checking your current line number while reading a document - it tells you where you are without changing position.
Fixed record size in random access is like apartments in a building - each apartment (record) has the same floor area (size), so you can calculate exactly which floor a specific apartment is on.
